title: Doorknocking that keeps working when the signal doesn't
excerpt: Concrete stairwells and rural dead spots shouldn't cost you conversations. Here's how the Uprise canvasser app records every door, no bars required.
body:
Every field organiser has a sixth sense for dead spots. The apartment block with concrete walls. The rural road where the bars vanish. The stairwell that swallows your signal whole. For years that's exactly where canvassing software fell over – you'd knock, tap a result, and watch a spinner turn while the data went nowhere.

We built the Uprise canvasser app so the signal is never the thing standing between a volunteer and a recorded conversation. Here's how it holds up when the network doesn't.

## The dead-spot problem is a data-loss problem

A dropped door isn't just an inconvenience. It's a hole in your data. If a volunteer knocks fifteen doors in a basement block and the app only saves the three that happened near a window, your support numbers are wrong, your follow-up list is wrong, and your reporting quietly lies to you.

So the app assumes the network will fail. Not as an edge case – as the normal state of a canvassing shift. Everything is designed to work first, sync second.

## An outbox that lives on the phone

When a volunteer records a door knock – the disposition, the survey answers, the support score – it doesn't get posted straight to a server and hope for the best. It gets written to an outbox stored right on the device, in the browser's own database.

That means:

- **Every knock is saved the instant it's tapped.** No spinner, no wait, no lost result.
- **The queue survives.** Close the app, lock the phone, walk into a lift – the queued knocks are still there when you come back.
- **Sync happens the moment there's signal again.** When the connection returns, the outbox flushes automatically. Volunteers don't have to remember to press anything, and organisers don't have to chase missing data at the end of the day.

A canvasser can work a full turf underground and surface to find every single door already on its way to the campaign.

## Route optimisation with zero bars

Here's the part that surprises people: the app doesn't just store data offline, it thinks offline.

Work out the best order to walk a list of addresses and you're solving a genuinely hard routing problem. Most tools do that on a server, which means the moment you lose signal, your smart route turns back into a random list. Ours runs the optimisation on the device.

The app uses a nearest-neighbour pass to build a sensible route, then a 2-opt pass to untangle the crossings and shorten it – all inside the service worker, with no network call at all. A volunteer standing on a corner with no reception can still re-order their remaining doors into the shortest sensible walk and get moving.

That's fewer steps, less backtracking, and more doors per hour – in exactly the places where connectivity is worst and canvassing is usually hardest.

## Install it like an app

The canvasser tool is a progressive web app, which is a technical way of saying: your volunteers install it to their home screen straight from a link, with nothing to download from an app store and no approval queue to wait on.

That matters more than it sounds. When you onboard a room full of volunteers on a Saturday morning, every extra step costs you people. "Open this link, tap add to home screen" gets everyone knocking in minutes. Once it's installed, it opens like any other app on the phone – full screen, ready to go, working whether or not there's a connection at that moment.

## What this means on a Saturday

Picture the shift you actually run. Twenty volunteers, mixed suburbs, patchy coverage, a couple of high-rise turfs nobody wants because "the app never works in there". 

With an on-device outbox and on-device routing, those turfs stop being a problem. Volunteers knock, record, and move on without thinking about signal. The high-rise gets covered properly for the first time. And when everyone comes back to the meeting point, the data isn't a guess reconstructed from memory – it's every door, in order, already synced.

Field work is hard enough. Losing conversations to a coverage map shouldn't be part of it.

Cut your turf, hand out the link, and let the app worry about the network.

title: Send ten thousand texts like you'd send one
excerpt: Personalisation that reads human, a live preview before you hit send, compliance handled for you, and one inbox the whole team shares. The Uprise outreach stack, end to end.
body:
Peer-to-peer texting works because it feels like a person reaching out, not a machine broadcasting. The trouble is that most tools make you choose between that human feel and doing it at scale. Uprise doesn't. Here's how the SMS console and the shared team inbox fit together, from the first send to the last reply.

## Personalisation that reads like a person wrote it

A text that opens "Hi FIRSTNAME" is worse than no text at all. So the console builds around personalisation tags – you drop in a supporter's details and the message assembles itself, per recipient, so every text lands as if it were typed for that one person.

And because a message can read fine in your head and badly on a phone, the console gives you a dual-channel live preview. As you write, you see exactly what the recipient will see, updating in real time. No guessing at line breaks, no surprise truncation, no sending ten thousand texts before you notice the tag didn't fill.

## Prove it before you send it

Confidence at scale comes from small checks done well. Before a blast goes anywhere near your list, you can:

- **Send yourself a proof.** Fire a real message to your own phone and read it the way a supporter would, on an actual screen.
- **Pick the right from-number.** The from-number selector lets you send from the number that fits the campaign, so replies land where you expect them.
- **Schedule it or send it now.** Line a send up for the moment your audience is actually looking at their phone, or push it straight away when the moment's live.

These aren't power-user extras. They're the difference between a send you trust and a send you hold your breath through.

## Compliance, handled

Every texting program lives or dies on getting opt-outs right. Get it wrong and you're not just annoying people – you're exposing the campaign.

Uprise runs an automated opt-out compliance check as part of the send, so the console is watching for the problems before you commit. Underneath it, an opt-out compliance ledger keeps the record: anyone who has opted out is automatically excluded from every future send, across the whole account. You don't maintain a suppression list by hand and you don't rely on a volunteer remembering. Someone says stop, and they're out – permanently, everywhere.

That's compliance as a default, not a checkbox you hope somebody ticked.

## One inbox the whole team shares

A blast is the easy half. The replies are where campaigns are won or lost, and a big send produces a lot of them, fast. Route those into a dozen personal phones and conversations get dropped, doubled, or lost.

The unified inbox puts every inbound message into one shared, claimable queue the whole team works from. It's built for a room of organisers moving quickly:

- **Claim and release.** An organiser claims a conversation to own it, so two people never reply to the same person. Done, or need to hand it on? Release it back to the queue.
- **Folders, stars and search.** Organise the flood, flag what matters, and find any thread in seconds.
- **Live, over the wire.** New messages stream in live – no refreshing, no wondering if you've missed something.
- **Audible alerts.** When a new inbound lands, the responder hears it. Nobody's staring at a silent screen waiting.

The result is a queue a whole team can share without stepping on each other – the digital version of a phone bank room where everyone can see the board.

## From send to conversation

The point of all of this is continuity. A supporter gets a text that reads like a person wrote it, because one effectively did. They reply, and a real organiser picks it up from a shared queue, in real time, with the compliance already taken care of.

That's the whole arc – personal at the top, personal at the bottom, and at a scale a small team can actually run. Write it, preview it, prove it, send it, and catch every reply in one place.

Good outreach was always a conversation. This just lets you have a lot of them at once.

title: Surveys that branch like a real conversation
excerpt: Skip logic, terminal branches, and four question types – the same survey working on the doors and over text, wired straight to canned replies and dispositions.
body:
A good conversation on a doorstep never runs down a fixed list of questions. It follows the person. Say you support the campaign and I'll ask if you'll volunteer. Say you don't and I'll ask why, gently. A survey that can't do that – that marches every person through the same rigid script – isn't listening, and people can tell.

Uprise surveys branch. Here's what that gives you.

## Skip logic and terminal branches

Every answer can change what comes next. With per-option skip logic, each choice a respondent makes can route them to a different question – so the survey adapts to the actual conversation instead of forcing the conversation to fit the survey.

And sometimes the right move is to stop. Terminal branches let an answer end the survey cleanly. If someone tells you they're moving out of the electorate next week, you don't need to walk them through five more questions – the branch closes the conversation on a natural note and moves on.

The effect is a survey that feels like it's paying attention, because it is.

## Four question types, one builder

Real conversations aren't all multiple choice, so the builder gives you the types you actually need:

- **Single choice** for a clean either/or.
- **Multi choice** when more than one answer is true.
- **Yes/no** for the quick reads that keep momentum up.
- **Text** for the things you'd never predict – the comment that tells you what the electorate is really thinking.

Mix them freely inside one survey. A yes/no can open the door, a single-choice can sort the response, and a text field can catch the detail that turns into your next talking point.

## The same survey on the doors and over text

Here's what ties it together: the survey you build works both on the doors and over SMS. One survey, two channels.

That matters because your program isn't one channel. A canvasser runs the survey face to face on a Saturday; the same instrument goes out over text to the people you couldn't reach in person. You're not maintaining two versions that slowly drift apart, and you're not comparing results from two different questionnaires and hoping they line up. It's the same questions, the same branches, the same data – however the conversation happened.

## Wired to canned replies and dispositions

A survey answer shouldn't just sit in a database. It should do something. So each option can be wired to the two things that make outreach move:

- **An automatic canned reply.** Choose an option and the matching response can fire back on its own – the right follow-up text, sent the instant someone answers, without an organiser typing a word.
- **A disposition.** Each option can map to a disposition, so the answer feeds straight into how you're recording and scoring that contact.

Set it up once and the survey stops being a form and starts being a machine. Someone taps an answer over SMS, the right reply goes out, and the contact is scored – all from the same choice.

## Build once, learn everywhere

The reason branching surveys are worth the care is that they're where your program learns. Every door and every text is a chance to find out where a person actually stands – but only if the instrument is good enough to follow the conversation and disciplined enough to record it the same way every time.

Build the survey once. Run it on the doors and over text. Let each answer trigger its own reply and its own disposition. What you get back isn't a pile of raw responses – it's clean, consistent intelligence about your electorate, gathered the same way no matter how you reached the person.

Stop making people answer questions in an order that suits your form. Build the survey that follows the conversation instead.

title: Turn every door knock into a targeting decision
excerpt: Custom dispositions on a five-point support scale, honest results, a real pace target, and turf that reshapes around what you learn. This is how field data becomes a plan.
body:
Data you collect and never use is just doors you tired out your volunteers on. The whole point of canvassing is that what you learn on Saturday changes where you knock next Saturday. That loop – knock, score, read, retarget – is the field plan. Here's how to run it in Uprise.

## The five-point scale is the spine of everything

Start with how you record a conversation. Uprise lets you define your own dispositions – your own outcome codes, in your own language – but every one of them maps to a single five-point support scale, from STRONG_SUPPORT through to STRONG_OPPOSE.

That combination is the important part. Your volunteers use codes that make sense for your campaign, and the system still knows exactly how supportive each one is. So "rusted on", "leaning our way" and "polite but no" all sort themselves onto the same scale automatically. You get local language on the doorstep and consistent, comparable data underneath. No translation, no arguing about what a "3" means.

## Read the results honestly

Once the scores are landing, the results give you three views that, together, keep you honest:

- **Disposition breakdown.** What actually happened at the doors – the raw outcomes, counted.
- **Five-point support distribution.** Where your contacts sit on the scale. This is your real support picture, not the one you were hoping for.
- **Contact funnel.** How many doors turned into conversations, and how many conversations turned into scored contacts. This is where you catch the problem early – a great support number means nothing if your contact rate is quietly collapsing.

The temptation in field work is to read only the number that flatters you. Looking at all three at once stops you doing that.

## Set a pace, not a wish

A target with no pace attached is a wish. Goals in Uprise track pace against target, so you can see whether today's rate actually gets you to the number by the date that matters.

Use it to make real decisions. Behind pace with three weekends left? That's not a moment for optimism, it's a signal to add a shift, recut turf toward higher-density areas, or move volunteers off low-yield lists. The tool tells you where you are against where you need to be, while there's still time to do something about it.

## Feed it back into the turf

Here's where the loop closes. What you learned changes where you go.

You can cut fresh turf on the map or straight from geographic areas, with live address-count estimates so you know how big a bite you're taking before you commit volunteers to it. Turn that into optimised walk lists – built on real walking metrics, grouped by street, and re-optimisable on demand – and assign or reassign them as your priorities shift.

And the support data you gathered doesn't just sit in a report. Roll it into audiences and segments, and target the right channel at the right people – the strong supporters you want at an event, the persuadables who deserve another conversation, the opposers you can stop spending volunteer hours on. The scoring you did on the doors becomes the targeting you do next.

## Export and act

When you need the data outside the platform – a coalition partner, a mailing house, a scrutineering plan – the results export straight to CSV. No screenshots, no retyping, no version that only lives in one organiser's head.

## The plan is the loop

A field plan isn't a document you write in January and file away. It's a loop you run every week: knock the doors, score each conversation on a scale that means something, read the results without flinching, check your pace, and recut the turf around what you found.

Do that consistently and your program compounds. Every weekend, your list gets sharper, your volunteers spend their time on doors that matter, and your support picture gets more real instead of more hopeful.

That's what winning a field program looks like from the inside – not one heroic weekend, but a loop that keeps tightening. Run the loop.

title: Your electorate, mapped before you knock a single door
excerpt: G-NAF addresses, ASGS geography, every electoral division and electorate polling on a choropleth – Australian civic data built into Uprise, ready to turn into turf.
body:
Most campaigns start with a spreadsheet and a prayer – someone's export of addresses, a boundary map from a PDF, and a lot of manual stitching before anyone can knock a door. Uprise starts with the map already built. The Australian civic data your campaign runs on is loaded in from day one. Here's what that means.

## The civic data is already loaded

You don't import Australia. It's there. Built into the platform:

- **G-NAF addresses** – the national address file, so the doors are real, not guessed.
- **ASGS statistical geography** – the Bureau's standard geographic framework, so your areas line up with how the country is actually measured.
- **Electoral divisions at every level** – federal, state and local, so you can work to the boundaries that matter for your fight.
- **Politicians, policies and referendum data** – the political context, in the same place as everything else.
- **Demographics** – so you know who lives where, not just where they live.

That's the groundwork most campaigns burn their first fortnight assembling. Here it's the starting line, not the first sprint.

## Polling you can actually see

Numbers in a table hide their own story. A choropleth tells it at a glance. Electorate polling in Uprise gives you the analysis and the map together:

- **Crosstabs** to slice the results and see how support moves across the groups you care about.
- **Regional choropleth maps** that shade the geography by the numbers, so the strong areas, the soft areas and the battlegrounds are obvious the moment you look.
- **Canvassing targets** derived from the polling, so the insight points straight at action instead of stopping at "interesting".

This is where data stops being a report you read and starts being a decision you make – where to send the volunteers, which suburbs to defend, which to fight for.

## Share it publicly

Good data is worth showing. Polling views can be made public, and they're embeddable – so you can drop the map and the crosstabs straight into a briefing, a website, or a partner's page without exporting screenshots that go stale the moment the numbers move.

That turns your data from an internal artefact into a campaigning asset. A live, credible picture of the electorate that supporters, media and coalition partners can see for themselves.

## From map to turf

The reason all of this sits in one platform is so the map becomes movement. Once you can see where your support is, you cut turf straight from geographic areas – with live address-count estimates as you go, so you know exactly how many doors you're committing volunteers to before you assign a single one.

No export, no re-import, no gap between the analysis and the action. You look at the choropleth, you pick the area, you cut the turf, and it's real addresses your canvassers can walk – because it was G-NAF underneath the whole time.

## Know the ground before you walk it

Campaigns lose weeks to work that should already be done: finding the addresses, drawing the boundaries, matching the demographics, making sense of the polling. Building all of that in isn't a convenience feature – it's what lets a campaign spend its scarce early time on strategy instead of data plumbing.

Open the map. Read the electorate. Cut the turf. The ground is already there waiting for you.

title: Build a volunteer team that actually shows up
excerpt: Invitations and roles, a calendar volunteers can read, shifts that stick, and a live action room with one-tap broadcast push. The organising stack that turns sign-ups into a team.
body:
A sign-up sheet isn't a team. Anyone who has organised knows the gap between the number of people who said yes and the number who are standing in the room on Saturday morning. Closing that gap is the whole job. Here's how Uprise helps you do it – from the first invite to the moment everyone's out on the doors.

## Invite, approve, assign

A team starts with getting the right people in with the right access. Uprise gives you role-based team access, so an organiser, a phone-bank lead and a data volunteer each see what they need and nothing they shouldn't.

Growing the team runs two ways:

- **Invitations** – bring someone in directly and set their role from the start.
- **Join-request approval** – let people ask to join, and approve them when you're ready, so an open door doesn't mean an unguarded one.

And because nobody should feel lost on day one, a getting-started checklist walks new members through their first steps instead of leaving them staring at a dashboard wondering what to do.

## A calendar volunteers can read

People show up when they know when and where to be. The calendar puts it all in one place – events, shifts, and ad-hoc entries together, so a volunteer sees the whole picture of what's on rather than piecing it together from three different messages.

For the things you want the public at, events come with a public RSVP page, so supporters can sign themselves up without an organiser fielding every reply by hand. And when you need the list – for door codes, catering, follow-up – events export cleanly.

## Shifts that stick

A calendar tells people what's on. Shifts turn that into who's doing what, when. Slotting volunteers into defined shifts is how a vague "I'll help out sometime" becomes a concrete "I'm on the 10am canvass" – and a named commitment is a kept commitment far more often than a good intention is.

Shifts also feed the rest of your program. They sit in the same calendar your volunteers already read, and they're the backbone your field plan schedules against.

## The live action room

Here's what changes the feel of a day in the field. When the shift is live, the action room shows you the team in motion – it refreshes your active canvassers roughly every ten seconds, so you're watching the operation as it happens, not reconstructing it afterwards.

And when you need to reach everyone at once, it's one tap. Broadcast push sends a message straight to every volunteer's phone – a target bump, a weather call, a "one more hour, we're nearly there", a change of plan. No group chat nobody's watching, no ringing round twenty people. One tap, everyone's phone, right now.

That's the difference between a scattered set of individuals knocking doors and a coordinated team that can turn on a moment. When the last hour matters, you can rally the whole field at once.

## Show up, and keep showing up

The hardest part of organising was never the enthusiasm on day one. It's the second Saturday, and the fifth. It's whether the person who signed up in a rush of feeling comes back when it's raining.

The things that build that are unglamorous: a clear role so people know they belong, a calendar they can actually read, a shift with their name on it, a checklist so their first day isn't a fumble, and a live room where they can feel the team around them and hear from a lead in real time. Put those together and sign-ups turn into a team – the kind that's standing in the room on Saturday, and back again the Saturday after.

Stop managing a list. Build the team that shows up.
